note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old male presents with burning urination. He reports no fever, flank pain, or testicular pain. No systemic symptoms reported. There are no known medication allergies. Pregnancy status is not captured.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not available. No laboratory results yet.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): UTI in male (complicated by definition)  
  - Differential: male dysuria—UTI vs urethritis (GC/CT) vs prostatitis  
  - Red flags considered: fever, flank pain, testicular pain—all denied

P:  
  - Disposition: Patient may be managed in urgent care or primary care setting with appropriate testing and follow-up.  
  - Plan: Recommend urinalysis and urine culture to confirm infection and guide antibiotic therapy. Also recommend STI testing (GC/CT) given differential including urethritis. Empiric antibiotic treatment can be considered after testing, guided by local resistance patterns and patient allergies. Advise hydration and symptomatic management.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care if fever, worsening pain, flank pain, testicular pain, or systemic symptoms develop. Follow up in 48–72 hours or sooner if symptoms worsen.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- sex_at_birth: male  
- fever: no  
- flank_pain: no  
- testicular_pain: no  
- age_years: 35  
- med_allergies: none  
- Pregnancy status is not captured
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
